Page 1

Jia et al. BMC Psychiatry (2025) 25:358 https://doi.org/10.1186/s12888-025-06806-3 BMC Psychiatry RESEARCH Open Access

Exploring the longitudinal relationships between non-suicidal self-injury and depressive symptoms in adolescents: a cross-lagged panel network analysis Qiannan Jia1,2†, Zhongying Wu1†, Bin Liu1†, Yuting Feng1, Wei Liang1, Danxia Liu3, Lei Song1, Caina Li2* and Qun Yang1*

Abstract

Background Non-suicidal self-injury (NSSI) and depressive symptoms are two major public health issues among adolescents. Moreover, they can often co-occur, which can lead to additional negative outcomes, such as suicidal behaviors. Therefore, this study aimed to explore the comorbidity patterns of NSSI and depressive symptoms and provide valuable insights for future interventions.

Methods This study examined the relationship between NSSI and depressive symptoms via a cross-lagged panel network (CLPN). A total of 866 Chinese junior high school students (39.80% girls, Mage = 12.73 years, SD = 0.43) completed measurements during three time points.

Results Comorbidity rates of NSSI and depressive symptoms ranged from 10.51 to 15.13% among the adolescents. Moreover, results of the CLPN indicated that somatic and retarded activity, depressive affect, and lack of positive affect were strongly and positively associated with multiple NSSI behaviors. Conversely, burning from the NSSI cluster could negatively predict somatic and retarded activity and depressive affect. In addition, somatic and retarded activity and depressed affect were bridge symptoms that linked the comorbidity between NSSI and depressive symptoms. Conclusion These results demonstrate a negative reinforcement feedback pathway between NSSI and depressive symptoms, which provides evidence for intrapersonal negative reinforcement of NSSI. Furthermore, these findings identify the core and bridge symptoms between NSSI and depressive symptoms, which can provide new ideas for subsequent intervention treatments.

†Qiannan Jia, Zhongying Wu and Bin Liu contributed equally to this work.

*Correspondence: Caina Li chinali7371@163.com Qun Yang yangqun1125@hotmail.com Full list of author information is available at the end of the article

Page 2

Keywords Depressive symptoms, Non-suicidal self-injury, Comorbidity, Cross-lagged panel network, Chinese adolescents

Introduction Non-suicidal self-injury (NSSI) and depressive symptoms are two major mental health issues prevalent in adoles­ cents [1–5]. NSSI refers to acts of direct and deliberate damage to body tissues without the intent to die, such as hitting, burning, and scratching [6, 7]. According to a review, its prevalence rate was approximately 14–18% among community adolescents. NSSI usually serves as a form of negative emotion regulation associated with depressive symptoms [8]. Hazardously, NSSI and depres­ sive symptoms are often comorbidity, which can lead to additional negative outcomes, such as suicidal behaviors [9]. For example, 47% and 19.7% of adolescences with major depressive disorder reported NSSI behaviors and a suicide attempt, respectively, over a 12-month period [10]. Therefore, identifying the patterns of comorbidity between NSSI and depressive symptoms is important owing to the compound risk. The network approach to psychopathology recently provided a novel perspective for studying the comorbidity of mental disorders. This study intended to explore the comorbidity between NSSI and depressive symptoms via a cross-lagged panel net­ work model (CLPN) with three-wave longitudinal data. Furthermore, we aimed to provide an empirical basis for future interventions.

demonstrated that NSSI predicted an increase in depres­ sive symptoms [15, 21].

These theoretical and empirical findings appears to be contradictory but actually reflects a complex dynamic between NSSI and depressive symptoms, and requir­ ing novel theoretical frameworks and methodological approaches to unravel their relationship [22]. The tra­ ditional approach assumes that symptoms reflect the presence of an unobserved latent entity-mental disor­ der [23, 24]. Furthermore, the symptoms are character­ ized by“local independence”[25], such as the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM) and International Classification of Diseases (ICD) proposed by the American Psychiatric Association [26]. Therefore, studies that examined the relationship between NSSI and depression only focused on the scale or diagnostic sum scores while ignored complex interactions at the symp­ toms-level [23]. However, interactions between symp­ toms are common and a fundamental phenomenon [27]. For example, depressive affect may influence adolescents’sleep and eating habits, which may exacerbate somatic symptoms [28]. Thus, ignoring the interactions between symptoms fails to provide an accurate understanding of their relationships.

Distinct from the traditional perspective, the network approach to psychopathology suggests that mental dis­ orders arise from dynamic causal interactions between the symptoms themselves [23, 29]. Different symptoms can activate and drive each other, which can lead to the emergence and maintenance of mental disorders [22, 30]. Therefore, use of this network model to explore the relationship between NSSI and depressive symptoms and their symptom-level interactions can help research­ ers further identify the comorbidity pattern. Further­ more, bridge nodes in the network model can also help researchers reveal the key symptoms that trigger their comorbidity, which can provide an effective guide for the intervention of mental disorders.

Abundant research has been conducted to investigate the patterns of NSSI-depressive symptom comorbid­ ity, however, the findings remain unclear. According to Four Factor Model (FFM) of which suggests that NSSI is maintained by four reinforcement processes: intrap­ ersonal negative reinforcement, intrapersonal positive reinforcement, interpersonal positive reinforcement, and interpersonal negative reinforcement. Among them, intrapersonal negative reinforcement refers to the indi­ vidual using NSSI to reduce their tension or other nega­ tive affective states [11]. Thus, the negative emotions in individuals who are depressed may drive NSSI. Several studies used a longitudinal model and reported that depressive symptoms predicted an increase in NSSI [8, 12–14]. However, the opposite may also be true, ado­ lescents’NSSI may lead to depressive symptoms [15, 16]. Based on the Interpersonal Theory of Depression [17], adolescents who engage in NSSI are often rejected by their peers and experience interpersonal dilemmas [18, 19], which triggers their depressive symptoms [3]. Moreover, adolescents with NSSI typically perceive their physical scars as a“marker of stigma or shame”and establish a negative cognitive model, which can exacer­ bate their emotional disorders, such as depressive symp­ toms [8, 15, 20]. Consistent with this, prior studies have Recently, several studies have used network analysis to investigate the comorbidity of NSSI and depressive symptoms. Niu [31] examined 248 clinical adolescents and found that‘frequent thinking of self-injury’was a crucial bridging symptom between NSSI and depression. Another study indicated that‘having a desire to harm myself and cannot stop’was a key symptom between NSSI and depression [32]. In addition, researchers used cross-sectional networks to examine the relation­ ships between NSSI, depression, and childhood trauma. Results revealed that depressive mood was directly related to NSSI [33]. However, these studies used clinical

Page 3

samples, which hinders the generalization of their find­ ings to community adolescents. Moreover, both adopted cross-sectional network analyses that failed to capture the dynamic nature of mental disorders and explore the interactions between the different nodes. A cross-lagged panel network (CLPN) integrates a network model with a cross-lagged model to examine the auto-regressive effects of symptoms and cross-lagged effects between the symptoms. This can shed light on the interaction of men­ tal disorders gradually at the symptom-level and capture the dynamic nature of psychopathology [15]. Therefore, use of a cross-lagged network to explore the relationship between NSSI and depressive symptoms at the symptomlevel can better reveal their interplay and identify the core symptoms to understand the pattern of comorbidity.

This exploratory study used a cross-lagged panel net­ work to examine the pattern of comorbidity between NSSI and depressive symptoms among community ado­ lescents. We aimed to explore the prevalence of NSSI and depressive symptoms among the adolescents and exam­ ine the comorbidity rate. Subsequently, we aimed to use a CLPN model to examine the temporal interact effect of NSSI and depressive symptoms for T1-T2 and T2-T3 and investigate how the symptoms affected each other. There­ fore, this study aimed to identify the interaction between NSSI and depressive symptoms at the symptom/itemlevel and identify the key symptoms that triggered their comorbidity.

Method Participants and procedure We recruited 890 junior high school students from two middle schools in Xi’an, China. Of these, 98% were of Chinese Han ethnicity, and all participants were Chinesespeaking. Data were collected at three time points, one year apart; the first time point was in 2015. At each mea­ surement wave, informed consent was obtained from the adolescents, their parents, and schools. Research assistants explained the research goal and instructions on how to complete the questionnaires. Subsequently, all participants completed a set of questionnaires, which took approximately 30 min, in their classrooms. Each student

received a gift as compensation. All the proce­

dures were performed in accordance with the ethical norms for scientific research. Finally, 865 participants (97% of the initial sample,39.80% girls, Mage = 12.73 years; SD = 0.43) completed all the surveys during the three time points. Little’s test [34] results revealed that miss­ ing data were missing completely at random (χ2 = 192.89, df = 162, and p = 0.05). In addition, no significant differ­ ences were observed between the attrition and final par­ ticipants regarding gender 55 ( χ2(1) = 1.559, p = 0.21), age (t(549) = 0.014, p = 0.99), socio-economic status (SES) (t(487) = 0.95, p = 0.34), NSSI at T1 (t(536) = 0.12, p = 0.91), and depressive symptoms at T1 (t(548) =-1.11, p = 0.27).

Measures Depressive symptoms Depressive symptoms were measured via the Center for Epidemiologic Studies Depression Scale (The CES-D Scale) [35] that measured adolescents’depressive symp­ toms in the past two weeks. Responses were rated on a 4-point Likert scale that ranged from 1 (rarely or none of the time) to 4 (most or all time). The CES-D included 20 items divided into four constructs: depressive affect (eight items, e.g., I was bothered by things that usually do not bother me), positive affect (four items, e.g., I was happy), somatic and retarded activity (six items, e.g., I did not feel like eating; my appetite was poor), and interper­ sonal (two items, e.g., People were unfriendly). Depres­ sive symptoms was measured at three-time points T1, T2, and T3. This study used four constructs of CES-D as nodes in CLPN, and the Cronbach’s alphas were 0.86, 0.86, 0.87 for depressed affect at T1, T2, T3 respectively. 0.74, 0.80,0.78 for positive affect at T1, T2, T3 respec­ tively. 0.73, 0.71, 0.74 for somatic and retarded activity at T1, T2, T3 respectively. 0.68, 0.72,0.70 for interper­ sonal at T1, T2, T3 respectively, showing good internal consistency.

Non-suicidal self-injury We used the Deliberate Self-Harm Inventory (DSHI) to assess the types and frequencies of self-harm behaviors among adolescents over the past six months [36]. The DSHI included nine items that represented a distinct form of NSSI, such as biting, cutting, burning, carv­ ing, and punching among others. A 6-point Likert scale was used to assess the frequencies of NSSI: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6 indicated never, one, two, three, four, five, or more times, respectively. We considered the occurrence of any type of NSSI as indicative of NSSI, regardless of frequency. In addition, NSSI was measured at three time points: T1, T2, and T3. Cronbach’s alphas were 0.92, 0.93, and 0.87 respectively, which demonstrated good internal consistency.

Demographic covariates Participants’gender, age, ethnicity, and subjective eco­ nomic status were obtained as covariates. Subjective eco­ nomic status was assessed via a drawing of a ladder with 10 rungs that represented where people stood in society [37].

Data analysis SPSS version 21.0 was used to calculate the descriptive statistics and correlations between the variables. The CLPN model were estimated via R 4.0.3. First, missing

Page 4

data were processed via exclusion of participants who had missing core values in all the three waves, such as for NSSI and depressive symptoms. R package“mice”was used to handle the missing data for demographic covariates at T1, T2, and T3. Missing values for NSSI and depressive symptoms were deleted list-wise. The prevalence of depressive symptoms, NSSI, and their Table 1 The items information of depressive symptoms and NSSI Variable Items M(T1) M(T2) M(T3) Depressive symptoms Depressed affect I was bothered by things usu­ ally don’t bother me 1.72 1.84 1.72 I felt that I could not shake off the blues even with help from my family or friends 1.50 1.59 1.53 I felt depressed 1.64 1.71 1.66 I thought my life had been a failure 1.57 1.54 1.55 I was fearful 1.57 1.53 1.47 I felt lonely 1.50 1.47 1.52 I had crying spells 1.65 1.58 1.60 I felt sad 1.70 1.68 1.72 I don’t felt I was as good as others 1.67 1.68 1.65 Positive affect I felt hopeful about the future 2.10 2.19 2.22 I was happy 1.95 2.09 2.20 I enjoyed life 2.14 2.20 2.39 Somatic and retarded activity My appetite was poor 1.59 1.61 1.47 I had trouble keeping my mind on what I was

DOI:ng

1.69 1.83 1.70 I felt that everything I did was an effort 1.63 1.65 1.56 My sleep was restless 1.84 1.81 1.78 I talked less than usual 1.74 1.79 1.73 I could not get“going”1.35 1.34 1.28 Inter­ personal relationship People were unfriendly 1.49 1.42 1.35 I felt that people disliked me 1.53 1.50 1.43 Nonsuicidal self-injury NSSI1 Punching self 0.31 0.37 0.26 NSSI2 Interference with wound healing 0.31 0.41 0.29 NSSI3 Cutting 0.28 0.37 0.20 NSSI4 Carving words or pictures into skin 0.25 0.41 0.23 NSSI5 Severe scratching 0.16 0.29 0.13 NSSI6 Rubbing sandpaper on skin 0.19 0.30 0.14 NSSI7 Sticking pins, needles, staples, rubbing glass into skin 0.21 0.33 0.18 NSSI8 Burning with cigarette, lighter or match 0.09 0.19 0.04 NSSI9 Biting self 0.24 0.35 0.24 co-occurrence rate at T1, T2, and T3 were analyzed. Second, the CLPN model was estimated using least absolute shrinkage and selection operator (LASSO) regu­ larization via the R package glmnet, and the regulariza­ tion parameter (λ) in LASSO regression was rigorously selected through 10-fold cross-validation, with the goal of minimizing the cross-validated prediction error (mean squared error, MSE). R package qgraph was used to visu­ alize the results. CLPN comprised autoregressive path­ ways for nodes and tests for cross-lagged effects between different nodes, the blue and red arrows indicate posi­ tive and negative relationships, respectively, and thicker lines denote stronger relationships between nodes. Vari­ able centrality was measured via the out Expected-Influ­ ence (out-EI) and in Expected-Influence (in-EI), which referred to the sum of the values of the outgoing and incoming edges connected to a node, respectively. Subse­ quently, bridge EI was also used to identify bridges nodes, which referred to the sum of a nodes’edges to nodes from other domains. We performed 5000 random boot­ straps of both“nonparametric”and“case-drop”types to assess the accuracy and stability of the networks. CScoefficient of > 0.5 indicated that the network was accu­ rate and stable [38].

Results Descriptive analyses The items and nodes information for depressive symp­ toms and NSSI are shown in Table 1. The prevalence of NSSI and depressive symptoms at three measurement time are presents in Tables 2 and 211 (24.36%) reported depressive symptoms at T1, 250 (28.87%) reported depressive symptoms at T2 and 209 (24.13%) reported depressive symptoms at T3; 210 (24.25%) reported NSSI at T1, 239 (27.60%) reported NSSI at T2 and 206 (23.79%) reported NSSI at T3. For the comorbidity of NSSI and depressive symptoms, 97 (11.20%) were reported at T1, 131 (15.13%) were reported at T2, and 92 (10.51%) were reported at T3. Table S1 presents the descriptive sta­ tistics and the bivariate correlations for all variables in this study. As expected, NSSI and the four constructs of depressive symptoms were positively correlated at T1, T2, and T3 time points.

Accuracy and stability of network The correlation stability (CS) coefficient revealed that the CLPN model was stable in the following respects in-Expected Influence (CS-coefficient = 0.361), out-Expected Influence (CS-coefficient = 0.749), and bridge Expected Influence (CS-coefficient = 0.749) for T1-T2 network. In-Expected Influence (CS-coefficient = 0.595), out-Expected Influence (CS-coefficient = 0.594), and bridge Expected Influence (CS-coefficient = 0.594) for T2-T3 network. Bootstrapped confidence intervals

around edge weights were small to moderate (see

CLPN model Supplementary Tables S2 and S3 present the adjacency matrix of the T1-T2 and T2-T3 cross-lagged panel net­ works, respectively.

Fig. S6 illustrates the autoregressive

edges for each symptom in each network. Nodes with the greatest auto-regression coefficients were Somatic and retarded activity (β = 0.29), Interference with wound healing (NSSI2) (β = 0.27), and Positive affect (β = 0.24) for T1-T2 network, Depressed affect (β = 0.42), Positive affect (β = 0.36), and Interference with wound healing (NSSI2) (β = 0.32) for T2-T3 network.

The cross-lagged network of NSSI and depressive symptoms is shown in

Fig. 1. The autoregressive edges

and β ≤ 0.05 were excluded to enhance the visual inter­ pretability of cross-lagged edges (see

Fig. S6 for fur­

ther details). In the T1-T2 network, the three strongest cross-lagged edges between NSSI and depressive symp­ toms were Somatic and Retarded Activity → Biting self (NSSI9)(β = 0.23),‘Somatic and Retarded Activity’→‘Rubbing sandpaper on skin’(NSSI6) (β = 0.22),‘Burning with cigarette, lighter or match’(NSSI8) →‘Depressed affect’(β = 0.14). In the T2-T3 network, the three stron­ gest cross-lagged edges between NSSI and depressive symptoms were‘Depressed affect’→‘Punching self’(NSSI1) (β = 0.20),‘Depressed affect’→‘Cutting’(NSSI3) (β = 0.15), and‘Positive affect’→‘Cutting’(NSSI3) (β = 0.13).

Centrality estimates are plotted in

Fig. 2. for in Expect­

edInfluence and out ExpectedInfluence, and

Fig. 3. for

bridge ExpectedInfluence. The two highest in-EI were‘Rubbing sandpaper on skin’and‘Severe scratching’for T1-T2 network,‘Interpersonal’and‘Severe scratching’for T2-T3 network, indicating they were susceptible to be influenced by other symptoms. The two highest out-EI were‘Somatic and Retarded Activity’and‘Rub­ bing sandpaper on skin’for T1-T2 network,‘Depressed affect’and‘Positive affect’for T2-T3 network, mean­ ing they had stronger predictive power for other symp­ toms. Moreover, the strongest bridge-EI symptoms were‘Somatic and Retarded Activity’for T1-T2 network and‘Depressed affect’for T2-T3 network.

Discussion To the best of our knowledge, this study is the first to explore the patterns of comorbidity between NSSI and depressive symptoms via a three-wave longitudinal data from a network perspective. We found that comorbid­ ity rates of NSSI and depressive symptoms ranged from 10.51 to 15.13% among the adolescents. Furthermore, we found the negative reinforcement feedback pathway between NSSI and depressive symptoms, that is, somatic and retarded activity and depressive affect, positively pre­ dicted NSSI. Conversely, NSSI could also negatively pre­ dict somatic and retarded activity and depressive affect. Both depressive affect and somatic and retarded activity acted as key bridges symptoms in this negative reinforce­ ment feedback pathway. These findings offered insight into the comorbidity of NSSI and depressive symptoms, as well as their underlying interaction pattern, which pro­ vides new ideas for subsequent intervention treatments.

In the T1-T2 network, We found that somatic and retarded activity could positively predict multiple NSSI behaviors. This finding was consistent with previous study which found somatic symptoms may impaired the link between body representations and self-protection which in turn lead to more NSSI behaviors [39]. The somatic and retarded activity dimension in the CES-D measured the degree of loss of appetite, sleep, and energy in adolescents. Poor sleep quality [40] and loss of appe­ tite [41–43] have been associated with NSSI in adoles­ cents. Moreover, Bohman [44] found that the duration and depth of depression were strongly correlated with somatic and retarded activity symptoms. Furthermore, an increasing number of somatic complaints implied a higher depression severity, which could further induce NSSI. In the T2-T3 network, both depressive affect and lack of positive affect could positively predict punching self and cutting, that is, adolescents with higher depres­ sive affect and less positive affect engaged in more NSSI. This finding supports the affect-regulation-based models of NSSI [11, 45, 46], such as Four-Factor Model (FFM). Previous studies reported that adolescents’negative affect increased their risk of NSSI [47–49]. Moreover,

Page 6

Fig. 1 The cross-lagged network of NSSI and depressive symptoms. Note: The autoregressive edges and β ≤ 0.05 were excluded to enhance the visual

interpretability of cross-lagged edges. The blue and red arrows indicate positive and negative relationships, respectively, and thicker lines denote stronger relationships between nodes depressive affect, a dimension of depression, was identi­ fied as the strongest predictor of suicide in adolescents [50]. This study expanded this finding via an examination of the predictive impact of depressive affect on NSSI. Fur­ thermore, our CLPN analysis revealed a dynamic shift in bridge nodes connecting depressive symptoms and NSSI between the T1-T2 and T2-T3 networks. Specifically, somatic and retarded activity served as bridge nodes in the T1-T2 network, whereas depressive affect emerged as the primary bridge node in the T2-T3 network. This shift may reflect the stage-dependent evolution of depressive symptoms during adolescence. In early adolescence, immature cognitive-emotional development limits the ability to articulate internal emotional states, leading to somatic symptom expression as the primary manifes­ tation of depressive symptoms [51, 52]. As cognitiveemotional functions mature, adolescents develop clearer awareness of negative emotions, resulting in depressive affect becoming the core symptom presentation [51, 52]. Consequently, the evolution of core depressive manifes­ tations may drive changes in bridge nodes linking depres­ sion and self-harm across developmental stages.

Page 7

Fig. 2 The in ExpectedInfluence and out ExpectedInfluence of CLPN. Note: in-EI refers to the sum of the values of incoming edges connected to a node

while out-EI refers to the sum of the values of outgoing ones. The higher in-EI value means the more predicted by other nodes. The higher out-EI value means the more influence to other nodes In this study, the nodes with higher in-EI were‘Rub­ bing sandpaper on skin’and‘Severe scratching’in the T1-T2 network, and‘Interpersonal’and‘Severe scratch­ ing’in the T2-T3 network. These findings indicate that different NSSI behaviors and interpersonal difficulties are are more likely to be affected by the combination of adolescents’depressive symptoms and NSSI nodes in CLPN model. Notably, nodes with the highest out-EI overlapped with the bridge nodes—specifically, somatic and retarded activity and depressive affect. This suggests that somatic symptoms and depressive affect may act as transdiagnostic mechanisms underlying the co-occur­ rence of depressive symptoms and NSSI.

In addition, we found that burning cigarettes, lighter, or matched cigarettes [NSSI8] negatively predicted somatic and retarded activity and depressive affect. This indicated that NSSI could alleviate adolescents’depressive affect and somatic and retarded activity. This finding also sup­ ported those of the intrapersonal negative reinforcement function of NSSI [11]. This finding, combined with the finding that adolescents’depressive affect and somatic and retarded activity predicted their NSSI, indicated the negative reinforcement feedback pathway between NSSI

Page 8

Fig. 3 The bridge ExpectedInfluence of CLPN. Note: bridge-EI refers to the sum of the value of all edges that exist between a node and all nodes which

from a different community and depressive symptoms. Moreover, depressive affect and somatic and retarded activity acted as key bridges symptoms in this negative reinforcement feedback path­ way. When adolescents experienced depressive affect and somatic and retarded activity, they may use NSSI to cope with these symptoms, which did help alleviate these symptoms. Thus, this negative reinforcement feedback pathway between NSSI and depressive symptoms implies that helping adolescents develop emotion regulation strategies to cope with depression is extremely important and could reduce and inhibit their maladaptive behav­ iors, such as adopting NSSI to alleviate depression.

other types of NSSI. Previous research also found that burning skin was a frequent type of NSSI [48, 56, 57]. Our finding further suggested that burning skin was the least frequent yet most severe practice that could influ­ ence other types of NSSI. Future research should exam­ ine the interactions between different types of NSSI and provide new perspectives for interventions.

Limitations and Future Directions This study explored the relationship between NSSI and depressive symptoms via the CLPN model with threewave data obtained from Chinese adolescents and pro­ vided potential treatment implications for intervention. However, some limitations should be noted. First, this study used self-report data rather than clinical diagno­ ses for NSSI and depressive symptoms. Hence, avoid­ ing self-reports bias as well as reflecting the relationship between NSSI and depressive symptoms among a clini­ cal diagnostic sample may be difficult. Future research should explore this relationship via clinical diagnostic samples. Second, the CLPN used in this study, similar to the cross-lagged panel model (CLPM), cannot dis­ tinguish between-and within-individual effects, which limited our ability to make causal inferences [58]. Future research should use the random intercept cross-lagged panel model (RI-CPLM) to investigate the relationship between NSSI and depressive symptoms. Third, our mea­ surement scales had different time windows. Specifically, the CES-D measured adolescents’depressive symptoms during the past two week while the DSHI assessed NSSI over the past six months. Future study should align the measurement timeframes across the variables. Fourth, although this study clarified the underlying comorbidity patterns of NSSI and depressive symptoms, we did not examine the risk factors, such as genes [59], environmen­ tal factors [5], and cognitive factors [60]. Future research Although this study found the negative reinforcement feedback pathway, the pattern was inconsistent between the T1-T2 and T2-T3 networks. Specifically, somatic and retarded activity was the strongest predictor of NSSI in the T1-T2 network, whereas depressive affect and lack of positive affect were the strongest in the T2-T3 network. This suggests that the relationship between NSSI and depressive symptoms is complex and requires further exploration. A possibility for this inconsistency may be age-related increases in the heterogeneity of NSSI and depressive symptoms [53, 54], which may change their relationship. Future studies should explore their asso­ ciation from a person-centered perspective. Another reason may be that adolescents are characterized by sig­ nificant physical and mental changes. Hence the relation­ ship between NSSI and depressive symptoms may also change dramatically. Changes in the co-occurrence rates between NSSI and depressive symptoms from T1-T2 to T2-T3 in this study may be a microcosm of the dynamics of their relationship [55]. Future studies should track and examine this relationship over longer periods.

Furthermore, we found that burning with cigarette, lighter or match [NSSI8] positively predicted multiple symptoms of depression and also negatively predicted

Page 9

should explore the influence of risk factors on the comor­ bidities of NSSI and depressive symptoms. Sixth, this study relied on an Chinese adolescents (39.8% females, Mage = 12.73 years; SD = 0.43) sample. Thus, generaliz­ ability of our findings to other country adolescents and other age might be limited. Future research should reveal whether these findings hold across country and age.

Conclution Despite these limitations, this study has some theoretical and practical implications. Theoretically, our study found that depressive affect and somatic and retarded activity played key roles in the negative reinforcement feedback pathway between NSSI and depressive symptoms. This finding supported those of the four-factor model (FFM) of NSSI, which assumed that adolescents’NSSI could be triggered by their negative emotions. Practically, the negative reinforcement feedback pathway between NSSI and depressive symptoms suggests that we may be able to interrupt their association by teaching adolescents to correctly cope with depressive affect and somatic and retarded activities. Overall, these findings provide insight into the comorbidity of NSSI and depressive symptoms as well as their underlying interaction patterns. More­ over, these findings provide new ideas for their subse­ quent intervention treatments.

Data collection: Qiannan Jia, Bin Liu, Zhongying Wu, Yuting Feng, Wei Liang, Danxia Liu, Lei Song.Analysis and interpretation: Qiannan Jia, Bin Liu, Zhongying Wu, Qun Yang. Drafting of the manuscript: Qiannan Jia, Bin Liu, Zhongying Wu.

Critical revision of the manuscript: Qun Yang, Caina Li. Role of

Funding：Qun

Yang. Approval of the final version for publication: all co-authors.

Funding

This work was supported by the National Social Science

Fund of China [grant

number 2022-SKJJ-B-057].

Acknowledgements: We are grateful to the adolescents who participated, and to the research assistants who assisted with data collection.

Data availability The datasets generated and/or analysed during the current study are not publicly available due to the raw data is used in other papers that are being published but are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

Declarations Ethics approval and consent to participate All procedures used in studies adhered to the Shaanxi Normal University committee’s ethical requirements, as well as the 1964 Helsinki Declaration and its subsequent revisions or comparable ethical standards. Informed consent to participate has been obtained from the parents or legal guardians of any participant under the age of 16.

Consent for publication Informed consent was obtained from all individual participants included in the study.

Competing interests The authors declare no competing interests.

Clinical trial number not applicable.
